old_claim.note: Horticulture provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The Gilgal I fig evidence ties early domestication to deliberate cultivation and vegetative propagation, making horticultural practice a direct enabler.

source_shape.source_locator: Kislev, Hartmann, and Bar-Yosef report early Neolithic parthenocarpic figs from Gilgal I and argue they were maintained by vegetative propagation.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source connects early domesticated figs to deliberate propagation and cultivation practice.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Horticulture remains enabling because the edge captures cultivation practice without claiming all plant domestication begins with the same fig propagation pathway.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: plant_domestication keeps horticulture as an enabling edge with primary-source support.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to cereal domestication only.
A separate vegetative_propagation node is added and should replace horticulture for the fig lineage.
A stronger source shows the Gilgal I figs were gathered rather than cultivated.
short_reason: Cultivation practice is sourced by the Gilgal I fig evidence.
